help.about.title: About QuickLingo
help.about.body:
What QuickLingo is
QuickLingo is a desktop assistant for Windows that gives you fast AI translation while you work. Type a word or phrase (or send text from another app via a global hotkey) and get the result in a compact window next to your browser, chat, video player, or document.

It is not meant to be “just another online translator”, but a workflow tool for specific situations: online meetings, TV series with subtitles, work correspondence, and language learning from real context.

How it works
1. Choose an AI model and translation direction on the main window.
2. Enter text and press Enter — or use a global hotkey for selection or clipboard.
3. QuickLingo builds the prompt (profile instructions, glossary, and optionally recent translations) and sends the request to your chosen provider.
4. The result appears in the window; it can auto-copy to the clipboard if enabled.
5. The translation is saved to local history for later use.

Key concepts
• Direction — a separate translation mode (e.g. Eng → Ukr for series vs Eng → Ukr for work). You can have multiple directions with the same language pair but different prompts and glossaries.
• Profile — AI instructions: response style, level of detail, output format. Each direction can have its own active profile.
• Glossary — your dictionary of fixed translations; attached to every request as a hint for the model.
• History — all saved translations stored locally in SQLite; search, tags, export, reopen.
• Learning — flashcards, review, Anki export from words you actually translated.

Typical use cases
• Meetings — translate a phrase from chat or slides without switching apps.
• Series — look up an unknown subtitle word, add it to the glossary, and keep the same meaning later.
• Learning — build a personal corpus from history and turn it into review cards.

Settings and data
Everything is configured via Tools → Settings: API keys, models, directions, profiles, features, glossary. API keys can be stored encrypted (Windows DPAPI). History and settings stay on your PC; text is sent to the provider only to fulfill each translation request.

Getting started
1. Add an API key (Settings → API keys).
2. Pick a model and direction on the main window.
3. Optionally tune profile, glossary, and features (hotkeys, auto-copy, always on top).

For details on specific features, see other items in this Help menu and the tooltips on the Features tab.
help.models.title: Models
help.models.body:
The big picture
QuickLingo does not run its own AI model — it sends your text and prompt to an external service (provider). A provider is a company or local server (Groq, Gemini, OpenAI, etc.). A model is a specific neural network on that provider (e.g. Llama 3.1 8B or Gemini 2.5 Flash).

On the main window you pick one model from the list. That model handles every translation until you switch it. History records which model was used.

API keys
Cloud models require an API key from the matching provider.

Where to configure: Tools → Settings → API keys.

Supported providers:
• Groq — fast Llama and other models; free tier available.
• Gemini — Google models; free API key available.
• OpenRouter — many models through one key; some free options.
• Mistral — Mistral AI models.
• DeepSeek, OpenAI, Anthropic — paid APIs for their models.
• Ollama — special case: you set a server URL (base URL, usually http://localhost:11434/v1) instead of a cloud key. An Ollama API key is only needed for remote or secured servers.

Keys are stored locally in QuickLingo settings. If “Encrypt API keys (Windows DPAPI)” is enabled (Features → Privacy), they are protected with Windows DPAPI.

Each field has a hint with a link to get a key. If the key for the selected model is missing, the status bar shows a warning.

Model list
Where to configure: Tools → Settings → Models.

This is the list shown in the dropdown on the main window and in Learning (AI corpus analysis).

• Order in the list = order in the dropdown (first item is at the top).
• Pick a provider on the left, then choose a catalog model or type an ID manually.
• Move Up / Move Down change order; Remove deletes from the list (at least one model must remain).
• Reset to defaults restores the starter set (Groq Llama + Gemini Flash).

The catalog includes popular models with provider labels. For Ollama, locally pulled models (ollama list) are added automatically.

Adding a model manually
If a model is not in the catalog, type its ID and click Add.

• Select the provider on the left — QuickLingo knows which API to call.
• Or use a prefix in the ID, e.g. gemini:gemini-2.5-flash or openai:gpt-4o-mini — the provider is inferred automatically.
• Without a prefix, the ID defaults to Groq unless it looks like gemini-, gpt-, claude-, etc.

How a model is used when translating
1. You select a model on the main window.
2. QuickLingo resolves the provider and loads the matching key (or Ollama URL).
3. The prompt is built (profile + glossary + other extras).
4. The request is sent with the model ID, profile temperature, and your text.
5. The response is shown; history stores the model and provider.

Temperature and response style come from the translation profile, not the model. The model is the “brain” (speed, quality, cost); the profile shapes how the answer is worded.

Choosing a model
• Quick word/phrase lookups — lighter models (8B, Flash, Flash Lite).
• Complex text, nuance, long explanations — stronger models (70B, Pro).
• Free to start — Groq and Gemini; OpenRouter has free models marked (free).
• Privacy and offline — Ollama on your PC (needs RAM and a pulled model).
• Paid APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek) — when you need a specific model or stable limits.

Tips
• Keep several models from different providers in the list and switch by task.
• After changing keys or the model list, click OK in Settings — the main window refreshes.
• If translation fails with an API error, check the key, provider limits/billing, and model ID.
• Model usage stats appear in Dashboard (Tools → Dashboard) when the corresponding History feature is enabled.
help.directions_profiles.title: Profiles
help.directions_profiles.body:
Why three tabs
Translation in QuickLingo is not a single “mode” — it is three layers:
• Direction — what you translate and in which context (a separate choice on the main window).
• Profile — how the AI should respond: prompt, format, level of detail, temperature.
• Usage — which profile is active for each direction by default.

Together this lets you have, for example, two Eng → Ukr directions — one for TV series and one for work — with different prompts, formatters, and glossaries.

Directions (Tools → Settings → Directions)
A direction is a separate translation mode with its own ID. It is not just a language pair: two directions can share the same source and target languages but have different labels and settings.

Direction fields:
• ID — internal key (Latin letters, no spaces), e.g. en-ua-series.
• Label — what you see on the main window, e.g. “Eng → Ukr (series)”.
• Source / target language — language code (en, uk, etc.); a hint for the prompt.
• Default profile — used if no other choice is saved for this direction.
• Enabled — whether the direction appears on the main window.

Add, Duplicate, and Delete make it easy to create a second direction by copying the first and changing ID and label.

Important: a new direction does not translate on its own — you must add it to a profile (see below) and write a prompt.

Profiles (Tools → Settings → Profiles)
A profile is a set of AI instructions. One profile can serve several directions: each direction gets its own tab with its own prompt and formatter.

What you configure in a profile:
• Name and description — for your own reference and dropdown labels.
• Temperature — model “creativity” (0.2 is steadier, higher is freer).
• Prompt — main instructions sent with every request for that direction.
• Formatter — how to present the result (cards, plain text, etc.).

For a profile to work with a direction, click Add direction and pick the one you need. A profile must include at least one direction to save.

On the main window, the Profile dropdown lists only profiles that have a prompt for the current direction.

Usage (Tools → Settings → Usage)
Here you pick the active profile for each enabled direction — the one applied on the main window when you switch directions.

Changing the profile on the main window is also saved here for the current direction. The direction’s Default profile is a fallback when nothing else is chosen yet.

Example: series vs work
Goal: both directions are Eng → Ukr, but series need colloquial style and slang explanations, while work needs formal brevity.

Step 1 — two directions (Directions tab):
• en-ua-series — label “Eng → Ukr (series)”, en → uk
• en-ua-work — label “Eng → Ukr (work)”, en → uk

Step 2 — profiles (Profiles tab). For example:

Option A — separate profiles:
• Profile “Series” — add only en-ua-series. Prompt: explain colloquial speech, idioms, slang; give subtitle context; unpack ambiguous words.
• Profile “Work” — only en-ua-work. Prompt: formal business tone; keep it short; no extra commentary; office correspondence terminology.

Option B — one profile “My translation” with two direction tabs and different prompts for series and work.

Step 3 — usage (Usage tab):
• Eng → Ukr (series) → profile “Series”
• Eng → Ukr (work) → profile “Work”

Step 4 — glossary (separate Glossary tab):
• For en-ua-series: pitch → present an idea, fine → monetary fine
• For en-ua-work: stakeholder → interested party, deliverable → work output

On the main window, switch direction — available profiles, glossary, and translation style change with it. History is tied to the direction too.

Typical workflow
1. Create or duplicate a direction.
2. Add it to a profile and write the prompt.
3. Pick the active profile in Usage.
4. Optionally fill the glossary for that direction.
5. Choose the direction on the main window and translate.

Tips
• Avoid changing direction IDs unless necessary — profiles, glossary, and history reference them.
• A disabled direction disappears from the main window but stays in settings.
• You can delete a direction only after removing it from all profiles.
• Use Move Up / Move Down on profiles to set the order in the main window dropdown.
help.formatters.title: Formatters
help.formatters.body:
What a formatter does
The model returns plain text — the prompt defines meaning, and the formatter turns the response into what you see in the Result field on the main window. Usually that is HTML cards with colors, spacing, and separators.

A formatter does not change the translation content — only presentation. History stores the raw model text; reopening a record applies the formatter again.

Where the formatter is set
The formatter is tied to profile + direction:
• Tools → Settings → Profiles → pick a profile → tab for the direction → Formatter field.

For series vs work you can pick different formatters: e.g. detailed cards for series and plain text for work.

When it runs
• After translation completes — the result passes through the formatter and is shown as HTML.
• With streaming enabled (Features → Streaming), you see plain text while waiting; formatting appears at the end.

Formatters tab (Tools → Settings → Formatters)
Create and edit formatters here, then select them in profiles.

List on the left — all saved formatters. Editor on the right for the selected one:
• ID and name — for your reference and profile dropdowns.
• Mode — Preset (quick) or Custom rules.

Preset mode
Built-in templates:
• Plain text — simple text with line breaks, no cards.
• UA→EN cards — cards for Ukrainian → English (variants, examples, context).
• EN→UA cards — cards for English → Ukrainian (word, explanation, translation, multiple senses).

Presets work best when the profile prompt asks the model for card-style output with ── separators or structure the preset expects.

Custom rules mode
A rules:v1 pipeline transforms text step by step. In the rules table you can add, reorder, and remove steps:
• escape_plain — simple HTML text.
• normalize_separators — unify separator lines.
• split_blocks / foreach_block — split into blocks and process each.
• format_ua_en_block / format_en_ua_blocks — UA↔EN card logic.
• wrap_card, wrap_document, line_style — wrappers and line styling.

Step parameters are JSON in the Params column. To start, click Duplicate to rules on a preset — you get an editable pipeline.

Preview
At the bottom — sample texts (дякую, fine, compel) and Update preview. Test the formatter before saving without running a real translation.

Prompt and formatter must match
They should work together. If the prompt asks for short plain text but the formatter is EN→UA cards, the output may look empty or broken.

Examples:
• Profile “Series” + en_ua_cards — the model returns expanded multi-sense cards; the formatter lays them out nicely.
• Profile “Work” + plain — the model returns a single line; the formatter shows text without extra decoration.

Typical workflow
1. Create or duplicate a formatter on the Formatters tab.
2. Check preview with a sample.
3. In Profiles, assign that formatter to the direction you need.
4. Make sure the prompt requests a compatible response format.

Tips
• New profiles often need only a preset; rules are for fine control.
• You cannot delete a formatter still selected in a profile — change the profile first.
• Add / Duplicate / Delete on the Formatters tab work like directions and profiles.
help.features.title: Features
help.features.body:
What this tab is
Tools → Settings → Features is where you turn QuickLingo capabilities on or off. Unlike directions or profiles, you do not define translation content here — you define app behavior: window, hotkeys, history, cache, learning, privacy.

Options are grouped into five sections. Most are simple enable/disable checkboxes. Where needed, there are numeric fields (cache TTL days, number of recent translations for context, etc.) or hotkey text fields.

How to use it
• Enable what you need and click OK or Apply.
• Hover over an option label for about one second — a popup shows a detailed description of that feature.
• Some changes (hotkeys, tray, single-line input) take effect after save; for hotkeys you may need to toggle the option off and on to restart the listener.
• Several capabilities only appear in Tools menus or context actions when the matching feature is enabled.

General
Day-to-day window comfort:
• Always on top — keep QuickLingo visible during series or meetings.
• Remember size, position, and zoom — no rearranging every session.
• Auto-copy result — paste translations into chat or documents immediately.
• System tray and autostart — app stays available in the background; closing the window hides rather than quits.
• Show/hide hotkey — bring the window back when hidden.

Input
Fast translation without focusing QuickLingo:
• Hotkey: translate selection — copy selected text from any app and translate.
• Hotkey: translate clipboard — translate what is already copied.
• Double Ctrl+C — alternative clipboard trigger.
• Replace in place — after hotkey translation, paste the result over the selection (Windows).
• Single-line input — Enter only translates; handy for single words.

Combos use pynput format, e.g. <ctrl>+<shift>+t.

Translation
Fine-tuning model requests:
• Response cache — repeat identical requests without API calls (TTL in days).
• History context — last N translations in the prompt for consistent wording.
• Glossary in prompts — attach your dictionary to requests (edit on the Glossary tab).
• Streaming — show text as it arrives; formatter runs at the end.
• Request queue — one translation at a time, no parallel API calls.

History and learning
The largest group — from saving translations to cards and Anki.

History and analytics:
• Auto-save translations — foundation for everything else; no history, cache, or dashboard without it.
• Search, filters, tags, CSV export — work with accumulated translations.
• Dashboard — activity chart and model breakdown (Tools → Dashboard).
• Meeting transcript — stitch history entries into chronological text by time gap.

Learning from your corpus:
• Star / phrasebook — mark important phrases in history.
• Difficult words — see what you translate most.
• AI corpus analysis — LLM suggests card candidates by tag.
• Card preview, Anki export, deck by tag/direction.
• Daily review, FSRS, streak, add selected word to deck from history.

Most of these unlock items in History and Learning windows; numeric params (top N words, review limit, corpus batch) are set on this same tab.

Privacy
• Encrypt API keys with Windows DPAPI — keys are not stored as plain text on disk.

Choosing a feature set
You do not need everything enabled. Typical bundles:

TV series:
Always on top, tray, selection hotkey, auto-copy, glossary, single-line input.

Work / meetings:
Clipboard or selection hotkey, auto-copy, cache, history context, request queue.

Language learning:
Auto-save, tags, phrasebook, word frequency, AI corpus, daily review or Anki.

Minimal:
Auto-save only + one model — add features as you need them.

How this relates to other tabs
• Glossary tab + “Use glossary in prompts” feature.
• Directions/profiles define content; features define how you use it comfortably.
• See other Help menu items for glossary, models, and directions in depth.

Tips
• If hotkeys stop working — check the combo, conflicts with other apps, and toggle the option to restart the listener.
• Cache and history context require auto-save enabled.
• Streaming and formatters: plain text while waiting, cards after completion.
• For full text on each checkbox — hover its label on the Features tab.
help.history.title: Request history
help.history.body:
What it is
Request history is a local log of all completed translations. Open it via Tools → Request history.

Entries are stored in SQLite on your PC (when “Auto-save translations to history” is enabled in Features). Each record includes date, direction, source text, result, model, tags, and a star flag.

Table and preview
• Click a row — the full result appears below.
• Double-click or “Reopen in main window” — send the text back to the main window with the same direction and profile.
• ★ column — phrasebook: mark important phrases (when “Star / phrasebook in history” is enabled).
• Tags column — labels for grouping (e.g. series name or project).

Search and filters
Depending on enabled features:
• Text search in queries and results.
• Filter by direction, model, tag, date range.
• “Starred only” — show starred entries only.

Export and analysis
• Export — CSV, JSON, or Markdown (respects active filters).
• Export transcript — stitch entries by time into meeting/conversation text (session gap is set in Features).
Tags
• Edit tags — for the selected record.
• Tag wizard — bulk add, replace, or remove tags on visible or selected rows.

Tags are useful for corpus work: later in Learning you can analyze entries with a given tag.

Extra actions
• Context menu in the result field — copy a fragment or “Add to deck” (when enabled in Features).
• Refresh — reload the table from the database.
• Clear — delete all history with no undo.

Links to other parts
• Translation cache and LLM context use history.
• Dashboard shows stats from saved records.
• Learning builds cards from history by tag.

If something is missing
Check the Features tab: search, filters, tags, export, phrasebook, transcript, and word frequency are separate toggles.
help.learning.title: Learning
help.learning.body:
What it is
Learning turns your translation history into study cards, spaced repetition, and quizzes. Open via Tools → Learning.

Three sections
1. Collect words — Create deck (AI analysis from tagged history) and Cards (review before studying).
2. Learn — Review (FSRS) and Quiz (self-test).
3. Statistics — progress, activity, and accuracy.

Recommended flow
1. On the main screen, translate with an optional tag (series, lesson, project). Untagged entries go to "Uncategorized".
2. Collect words → Create deck: pick tag or Uncategorized, direction, preview locally, confirm, Run AI analysis. Cards are saved automatically.
3. Check Cards, then Learn → Review due cards.
4. Learn → Quiz to test recall; use "Review these words" for mistakes.

Review
Pick a deck, Start review, rate Again / Hard / Good / Easy (FSRS when enabled). Interval previews appear on grade buttons.

History shortcut
Request history → "Create deck from tag" opens Learning with the current tag filter pre-selected.
help.dashboard.title: Dashboard
help.dashboard.body:
What it is
Dashboard is a quick analytics view of your translation history. Open via Tools → Dashboard.

You need saved translations (“Auto-save translations to history” in Features). With no records, charts stay empty.

Top summary row
• Total translation count.
• Breakdown by direction (when records exist).
• Review streak — when streak is enabled in Features and you review in Learning.

Activity chart (left)
Bar chart: how many translations were saved per day over the last 30 days.

Helps spot when you translate most — during series, meetings, or study sessions.

Toggle: Features → “Dashboard: activity chart”.

Model usage (right)
Pie chart: share of each LLM model among saved translations. Top models are shown separately; the rest grouped as “Other”.

Useful to see which providers you rely on and API cost drivers.

Toggle: Features → “Dashboard: model breakdown”.

Refreshing
Data is read from the local history database when the window opens. After new translations, close and reopen Dashboard — there is no separate Refresh button.

If a chart is missing
Check that the matching Dashboard options are enabled in Features. If both are off, only the text summary at the top remains.
help.glossary.title: Glossary
help.glossary.body:
What it is
The glossary is your personal dictionary of fixed translations. For each entry you set a source term and the exact target wording you want the AI to use.

This is not automatic find-and-replace in the app. QuickLingo does not rewrite your text before sending it. Instead, every time you translate, the glossary is attached to the request sent to the model — it becomes part of the prompt as instructions like “translate X as Y”.

How it reaches the model
When you press Enter to translate, QuickLingo builds the full prompt for the AI:
1. The base instructions from your profile (Detailed, Concise, etc.)
2. Plus a glossary block with your terms for the current direction, for example:
   Use these glossary terms when applicable:
   - fine → monetary fine (not “good” or “okay”)
   - bug → insect (not “software error”)
3. Plus any other enabled extras (e.g. recent translations for consistency)

The model reads this block together with your input word or phrase and tries to follow your glossary.

How to add terms
1. Open Tools → Settings → Glossary.
2. Choose the translation direction (e.g. Eng → Ukr).
3. Enter Source term and Target term.
4. Click Add term. The pair appears in the table below.
5. Click OK to save.

Entries are stored per direction. Terms added for Eng → Ukr are not used when you translate Ukr → Eng — create a separate list if needed.

When the glossary is used
• The feature “Use glossary in prompts” must be on (Settings → Features → Translation). It is disabled by default — turn it on after you add terms.
• The direction selected on the main window must match the glossary direction you edited.
• If the glossary is empty for that direction, nothing extra is sent.

Why it helps
• Many meanings — one English word can translate different ways. The glossary locks the sense you care about (e.g. watching a series: pitch = present an idea, not “pitch” as a sports field).
• Consistency — the same character or topic uses the same wording across sessions.
• Slang, names, jargon — you choose the rendering instead of leaving it to chance.

Important to know
• The glossary is a strong hint to the AI, not a hard rule. The model usually follows it, but may occasionally ignore or soften an entry — especially for very long or ambiguous glosses.
• Short, clear pairs work best. You can add a brief clarification in the target field if needed.
• To stop sending glossary hints entirely, turn off “Use glossary in prompts” in Features.

Typical workflow for TV series
You hear a word in subtitles, look it up once, add the pair to the glossary, and from then on every translation request for that direction includes your preferred meaning.
history.title: Request history
history.detail_placeholder: Select a row in the table…
history.clear_message:
Are you sure you want to clear all request history?
This action cannot be undone.
settings.title: Settings
settings.interface.note: Changes apply immediately when you click Apply or OK.
settings.features.translation_response_c: Reuse a recent identical translation without calling the API.
settings.features.ui_system_tray_note: Closing the window hides to tray instead of quitting.
settings.features.ui_autostart_note: Adds QuickLingo to the Windows startup programs.
settings.features.hotkey_note: Example: <ctrl>+<shift>+t. Requires restart of hotkey listener (toggle feature).
settings.features.input_tutor_capture_no: Windows: captures typing into QuickLingo while focus is in Meet, a video player, or another app. Toggle via the Global input button or hotkey below.
settings.features.input_replace_in_place: After translating selection, paste result back (Windows only).
settings.features.learning_ai_corpus_ana: Analyze tagged history with LLM to build flashcards.
settings.features.corpus_card_prompt_pla: Leave empty to use the built-in template. The button below inserts it for editing. Placeholders: {tag}, {direction}, {direction_rules}, {items}
settings.features.privacy_encrypted_keys: Keys are encrypted for this Windows user account. Re-enter keys after OS reinstall.
settings.features.help_missing: No detailed description is available for this option yet.
settings.features.help.ui_always_on_top:
Keeps the QuickLingo window above other applications so you can see translations while working in another app.

Useful when translating side-by-side with a browser, editor, or chat. The window stays visible until you turn this off or minimize the app.
settings.features.help.ui_remember_geome:
Saves the window size and screen position when you close QuickLingo and restores them on the next launch.

Also applies to Tools windows (History, Learning, Dashboard, Settings), including table column widths where available.

If you move the app to a second monitor or resize it for your workflow, you won't need to arrange it again each time.
settings.features.help.ui_remember_zoom:
Remembers the text zoom level (font scale) in the main translation view between sessions.

Helpful if you prefer larger text for readability or a compact layout on a small screen.
settings.features.help.ui_auto_copy_resu:
After each successful translation, automatically copies the translated text to the Windows clipboard.

You can paste the result into any app without selecting and copying manually. The original clipboard content is replaced with the translation.
settings.features.help.ui_single_line_in:
Replaces the multi-line input box with a single-line field.

Press Enter to translate. For longer text with line breaks, leave this option off and use Shift+Enter for new lines.
settings.features.help.ui_system_tray:
Shows QuickLingo in the system tray (notification area). Closing the main window hides the app instead of quitting it.

Use the tray icon or the show/hide hotkey to bring the window back. Global hotkeys can keep working while the window is hidden.
settings.features.help.ui_autostart:
Starts QuickLingo automatically when you log in to Windows.

The app launches in the background according to your tray and window settings. You can disable this anytime without uninstalling.
settings.features.help.history_auto_save:
Saves every completed translation to the local history database.

Records include source text, result, direction, provider, and timestamp. Disable if you don't want translations stored on disk.
settings.features.help.history_search:
Enables text search across saved translation history.

Find past translations by words in the source or result. Search works together with filters and tags when those features are enabled.
settings.features.help.history_filters:
Adds filters in the History window: date range, direction, provider, starred items, and tags.

Narrows long history lists so you can review or export only the records you care about.
settings.features.help.history_export:
Lets you export history records to a CSV file from the History window.

Useful for backups, spreadsheets, or sharing a subset of translations. Export respects active filters when applied.
settings.features.help.history_dashboard:
Shows a bar chart of how many translations you saved per day over the last 30 days.

Open via Tools → Dashboard. Helps spot study or work patterns in your corpus.
settings.features.help.history_model_sta:
Shows a pie chart of which LLM models were used for your saved translations.

Useful to see provider mix and cost drivers. Displayed in Tools → Dashboard.
settings.features.help.history_tags:
Allows assigning tags to history entries and filtering by tag.

Tags help group translations by project, topic, or language pair. Use Tag wizard in History to add, replace, or remove tags on all visible or selected records.
settings.features.help.history_meeting_t:
Builds a chronological transcript from history entries within a configurable time gap.

Entries closer together than the session gap are treated as one meeting or conversation. Export combines them into a readable document.
settings.features.help.learning_phrasebo:
Adds a star (phrasebook) marker on history items for quick access to important phrases.

Starred entries can be filtered in History so you can build a personal phrasebook from real translations you use.
settings.features.help.learning_difficul:
Detects words or phrases that appear often or may be hard based on your history patterns.

Use this as a preview before generating flashcards or focusing study on recurring trouble spots.
settings.features.help.learning_ai_corpu:
Uses an LLM to analyze a batch of tagged history and suggest vocabulary for flashcards.

Configure max candidates and batch size to balance quality, speed, and API cost. Results appear in the Learning window for review before saving cards.
settings.features.help.learning_anki_pre:
Shows generated flashcards inside QuickLingo before you export or study them.

Lets you edit or discard cards so only useful items reach Anki or daily review.
settings.features.help.learning_anki_exp:
Exports learning cards to Anki as a ready-to-import .apkg package or CSV (front/back columns).

For .apkg: File → Import in Anki. For CSV: File → Import. Deck name and tags follow your tag or direction settings when deck scope is enabled.
settings.features.help.learning_deck_sco:
Organizes cards into decks or scopes by history tag and/or translation direction.

Keeps vocabulary separated by project or language pair instead of one mixed deck.
settings.features.help.learning_daily_re:
Runs spaced-repetition review inside QuickLingo.

With FSRS off: Again / Good (simple intervals). With FSRS on: Again / Hard / Good / Easy using the FSRS algorithm. The daily limit caps due cards per session.
settings.features.help.learning_srs_revi:
Switches daily review from simple Again/Good intervals to full FSRS scheduling with four grades.

Requires Daily review to be enabled. Card scheduling state is stored locally in the learning database.
settings.features.help.learning_streak:
Tracks consecutive days with at least one completed review.

Motivates regular practice. The streak resets if you skip a day without finishing the minimum review.
settings.features.help.learning_extract_:
Adds a context action in History to send a selected word or phrase into a learning deck.

Quick way to turn a translation you just looked up into a flashcard without retyping.
settings.features.help.translation_respo:
Reuses a recent identical translation from history instead of calling the API again.

Matches source text, direction, and relevant settings. Cache entries expire after the configured TTL in days.
settings.features.help.translation_conte:
Sends the last N translations from history as context to the LLM for more consistent wording.

Helps with ongoing documents or chats where terminology should stay aligned across segments.
settings.features.help.translation_gloss:
Injects your custom glossary terms into translation prompts so fixed terms translate the same way every time.

Manage entries on the Glossary settings tab. Applies when the active direction matches a glossary rule.
settings.features.help.translation_strea:
Shows translation text as it arrives from the LLM instead of waiting for the full response.

During streaming the result appears as plain text; formatting is applied when complete. Cache hits are still instant.
settings.features.help.translation_reque:
Processes translation requests one at a time in order instead of running several API calls in parallel.

Reduces rate-limit errors and keeps results predictable when you trigger many translations quickly.
settings.features.help.input_global_hotk:
Global hotkey that copies the current text selection in any app and translates it in QuickLingo.

Configure the key combo below (pynput format, e.g. <ctrl>+<shift>+t). Toggle this option off and on to reload the listener after changing the combo.

Global hotkey that translates whatever is currently on the clipboard without selecting text.

Useful when copy is easier than a selection shortcut. Uses the same combo format as the selection hotkey.
settings.features.help.input_double_ctrl:
Detects two Ctrl+C presses in quick succession and starts a clipboard translation.

Works in apps where a dedicated hotkey is awkward but double-copy is natural. Does not replace normal single Ctrl+C behavior.
settings.features.help.input_tutor_captu:
Captures typing at the OS level and sends it to QuickLingo even when focus is in Google Meet, mpv/VLC, a browser, or any other app.

Toggle via the Global input button on the main window, this checkbox, or a global hotkey (default Ctrl+Alt+G). While active, plain letters do not reach the focused app; Ctrl/Alt/Win shortcuts pass through.
settings.features.help.input_replace_in_:
After translating selected text via hotkey, pastes the translation back into the original field (Windows only).

Simulates select → translate → paste in one step. Some apps may block simulated input for security.
settings.features.help.privacy_encrypted:
Stores API keys encrypted with Windows DPAPI for the current user account.

Keys are not saved as plain text on disk. After an OS reinstall or new Windows profile, you must enter keys again in Settings → API Keys.
learning.analysis_disabled: AI corpus analysis is disabled in Settings → Features.
learning.empty_untagged_body: Translate on the main screen without a tag. Words will appear here as "Uncategorized".
learning.empty_tag_body: Translate on the main screen with tag "{tag}" to build this deck.
learning.flow_hint_collect: Step 1: Choose a tag (or Uncategorized) and run AI analysis to create a deck.
learning.flow_hint_cards: Step 2: Review the generated cards before studying.
learning.flow_hint_review: Step 3: Review due cards — harder words appear more often (FSRS).
learning.flow_hint_review_done: Review done for today. Test yourself in Quiz.
learning.flow_hint_quiz: Step 4: Quiz checks active recall. Repeat weak words in Review.
learning.card_context_placeholder: One English example sentence per line
learning.onboarding.title: How Learning works
learning.onboarding.step1_body: On the main screen, translate words and phrases while watching a series (Eng → Ukr) or in lessons/meetings (Ukr → Eng). Add an optional tag — e.g. a series or project name.
learning.onboarding.step1_body_standalon: Use QuickLingo to translate and tag words on the main app. This Learning window turns tagged history into study decks.
learning.onboarding.step2_body: Open Collect words → Create deck. Pick a tag (or Uncategorized), choose direction, preview locally, then Run AI analysis. Cards are saved automatically.
learning.onboarding.step3_body: Review (spaced repetition) → Quiz (self-test) → Statistics. After Quiz, review weak words with one click.
learning.no_corpus: No records match this corpus filter
learning.review_idle_hint: Choose a mode and press Start review.
learning.review_done_title: All cards for today are done!
learning.review_done_subtitle: Come back tomorrow to reinforce what you learned.
learning.review_cram_hard_empty: No cards rated Again or Hard today.
learning.review_cram_train_empty: This deck has no cards to practice.
learning.quiz_idle_hint: Ready for a {count}-question quiz ({available} eligible words).
learning.quiz_no_words: No eligible words in this deck filter.
learning.quiz_no_words_missing_examples: No quiz-ready words — {missing} cards are missing English examples.
learning.quiz_disabled: Quiz is disabled in Settings → Features.
learning.quiz_question_edit_few_choices: Only {count} valid choices (minimum 4 recommended). Save anyway?
learning.quiz_question_regen_hint: e.g. distractors too similar, need more verbs…
learning.quiz_generation_partial: Generated {ready}/{total} cards ({missing} still incomplete). {failed} question rows failed validation — tap Generate again to retry missing types.
learning.quiz_generation_cancelled: Generation cancelled. {ready}/{total} cards ready ({missing} still incomplete). Tap Generate to continue.
learning.quiz_deck_ready: All {ready} eligible cards have quiz questions.
learning.quiz_not_ready: Generate questions first: {ready}/{needed} cards ready ({missing} still need questions).
learning.quiz_select_deck: Select a deck to prepare or start a quiz.
learning.quiz_no_eligible_cards: No quiz-eligible cards in this deck.
learning.quiz_idle_partial_ready: {missing} eligible cards still need generated questions.
learning.quiz_fix_cancelled: Fix cancelled. {fixed} of {total} cards were updated.
learning.quiz_fix_partial: Fixed {fixed} of {total} cards ({failed} could not be fixed).
learning.quiz_ineligible_hint: {count} cards need better English examples before quiz generation.
learning.quiz_idle_hint_multi: Ready for a {count}-question quiz from {decks} decks ({ready} ready words).
learning.progress_no_cards: No cards yet — run corpus analysis to build a deck.
learning.progress_no_activity: No activity in the last 26 weeks.
learning.progress_disabled: Progress dashboard is disabled in Settings → Features.
learning.progress_accuracy_reviews_only: Based on review sessions only (no quiz data yet).
learning.ai_deck.tag_invalid: Tag must be 2–40 characters: lowercase letters, digits, _ or -.
learning.ai_deck.duplicate_message: A deck with tag "{tag}" already exists for this direction. Add new cards to it?
learning.ai_deck.no_model: No AI models configured. Add models in Settings → Models.
settings.features.learning_ai_deck_gener: Generate new vocabulary decks from the Cards tab.
settings.features.quiz_prompt_placeholde: Empty = built-in template. Use Reset to insert defaults. Placeholders: {english}, {ukrainian}, {definition}, {hint_pos}, {examples}, {fill_sentence}, {blanked_sentence}, {choices_count}
dashboard.title: Dashboard
settings.api_keys.hint_ollama: Install Ollama, run ollama serve, then ollama pull llama3.2
settings.api_keys.hint_ollama_key: Only needed for remote or secured Ollama instances.
settings.api_keys.note: Keys are stored locally in your QuickLingo settings file.
settings.models.intro: Choose which models appear in the model dropdown on the main window and in Learning. Order matches the dropdown order. Filter by provider, pick from the list, or type a model ID manually.
settings.models.add_placeholder: Model ID or pick from list
settings.models.error_duplicate: This model is already in the list.
